segments.s611-g25: به here begins the phrase and functions idiomatically as an introductory preposition ('to/for') often used in epic Persian to introduce or spotlight someone — in sense it yields an existential/introducing nuance ('there was' / 'for a ...'). مردی is indefinite singular ('a man'), marked by the -ی suffix. تواندر is an older/poetic adjectival form meaning 'powerful, capable' and qualifies مردی. زمانه نوی is a genitival/locative phrase 'the new age' with زمانه (noun) followed by the postpositive adjective نوی ('new'); together the phrase means 'a powerful man of/in the new age.'
segments.s611-g26: که is a relative pronoun ('who/that') linking this clause to the preceding noun phrase (مردی). The repeated هم ... و هم ... و هم is the correlative/emphatic listing ('both ... and ... and ...'). شاه is the basic noun 'king.' خسرو here functions as a royal title/epithet ('sovereign' / 'king') rather than necessarily a proper name. گوی is a poetic noun meaning 'orb/sphere' (i.e. the realm or world) so the trio lists overlapping royal roles/titles; note there is no finite verb in this line — this nominal relative clause is left open/elliptical and the verb or further qualification follows later in the poem.
segments.s611-g17: بزد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of زدن ('to strike/shoot'); it introduces the action. تیر is the direct object 'arrow'. بر پشت is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on the back (of)', where بر marks location. آن گور نر is a noun phrase 'that male gūr' (here gūr = onager); دِمonstrative آن precedes the noun and the adjective نر follows it (typical Persian order for such modifiers).
segments.s611-g18: گذر کرد is the compound verb 'passed' (گذر + کردن) in past tense; the logical subject is the subsequent noun phrase پیکان و پر ('the arrow and the feather'), which in Persian can follow the verb. بر marks what was passed over/through ('over the gūr'). گور is again 'onager'; پیکان و پر coordinate the arrow and its feather/fletching — together they are said to have passed over/through the animal.
segments.s611-g19: نر و ماده را is a definite object phrase 'the male and the female' marked by the object marker را. هر دو 'both' emphasizes that both were affected. بر هم here means 'against one another / together' (they were effectively joined by the shot). بدوخت is the past of دوختن ('to sew/stitch') used figuratively — 'stitched/fastened together' — i.e. pierced through both.
segments.s611-g20: دل لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the host/army'. از زخم او 'from/because of his wound' gives the cause. بر فروخت is a verbal phrase where فروخت (here intransitive) means 'was kindled/ablaze' or 'glowed'; the prefixed بر intensifies or frames the action — together conveying 'were set aflame' (i.e. hearts were stirred/ignited) by his wound.
segments.s611-g21: ز لشکر 'from/among the host' uses the poetic preposition ز ('from'). هرانکس که ... دید is a free relative clause 'whoever/anyone who saw that wound' (هرانکس = 'whoever', که introduces the clause). آن زخم دید places the verb دید ('saw') after the object, a common classical word order.
segments.s611-g22: بران here means 'thereon/to that (one)' or 'upon that matter' and links the praise to the wound/occasion. شهریار is 'the king/sovereign'. آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise' or 'bravo', and گسترید is the past of گستراندن 'to spread/extend' (3rd pl.); together they mean 'they spread/uttered praise (to) that king.' The subject (they) is omitted but understood from the previous clause (members of the host).
segments.s611-g23: که introduces a wish/exhortation here. چشم بد 'the evil eye' is the subject of the wish. از فر تو 'from your farr' (فر = royal glory/radiance) indicates what it should be distant from. دور باد is an optative formula: دور 'far' plus باد, the optative particle 'may', yields 'may [it] be far'.
segments.s611-g24: همه روزگاران 'all the days/ages' is the period being blessed. تو marks possession 'your'. سور 'feast/banquet' is the good fortune wished for, and باد again is the optative 'may' — together 'may all your days be a feast' (i.e. may you enjoy continual celebration/festivity).
segments.s611-g9: همی is an archaic/progressive marker meaning 'keeps on' or 'continues to'. پوست کند is a verb phrase 'tears/peels the skin' with کند as the present/past-form verb 'does'. The sequence این ازآن آن ازین is a reciprocal, elliptical construction: 'this (one) [peels the skin] from that, that from this', where ازآن/ازین ('from that'/'from this') indicate the source of the skin being torn.
segments.s611-g10: ز introduces source or cause ('from/through'), خونشان is 'their blood' (خون + possessive شان), and شده is the intransitive/ stative 'has become'. لعل روی زمین literally 'the ruby of the surface of the earth' means 'the earth's surface became ruby‑red' — the word order puts the resulting state (لعل) before the location (روی زمین).
segments.s611-g11: همی بود is a progressive/persistent past construction ('kept on being/doing'); بود is the past-tense copula. تا introduces the temporal limit 'until', and the phrase گور نر ('male onager') is the subject of the following clause, with نر as the masculine qualifier after گور.
segments.s611-g12: به مستی is an adverbial phrase of manner ('in drunkenness/frenzy'), where به marks the manner. جدا شد is a common intransitive phrase 'became separated' (شد = 'became'), and یک از یک دگر is an idiomatic expression 'one from the other' = 'from each other / separated from the other'.
segments.s611-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. پیروز شد is the verbal phrase 'became victorious', and the noun phrase نره گور دلیر ('the brave male onager') follows as the subject; note the adjective دلیر comes after the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s611-g14: یکی ماده را is a definite/particular object ('one female' with را marking it as the definite/direct object). اندر آورد is a compound verb 'brought in/put under' and زیـــــر following the verb gives the directional/resultative complement 'under(beneath)'. The whole means 'he brought/put one female beneath (him)'.
segments.s611-g15: به زه is an instrumental/qualifying prepositional phrase 'with (its) string' (به marking accompaniment/instrument), داشت is the past 'had/held', and جنگی کمان is a noun phrase 'war‑bow' with the adjective جنگی qualifying کمان. The order puts the instrument first (typical in poetic diction) followed by the possessed object and possessor (داشت بهرام = 'Bahram had/held').
segments.s611-g16: بخندید is the main verb 'he laughed'. چون introduces a temporal clause here ('as/when'), followed by گور شد شادمان 'the onager became joyful' (شد = 'became', شادمان = 'glad/joyful'), so the subordinate clause gives the occasion for Bahram's laugh.
segments.s611-g1: به هشتم is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘on the eighth (day)’ placed before the verb for poetic emphasis. بیامد is the past‑tense, third‑person singular of آمدن ‘to come’ and is the main verb. به دشت شکار is a prepositional phrase ‘to the hunting plain’ (دشت ‘plain’ modified by شکار ‘hunting’); word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but functions as the goal of بیامد.
segments.s611-g2: خود here is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun meaning ‘himself’ or ‘his own’, linking back to the subject of بیامد. و simply connects the next phrase. روزبه is a proper name. با introduces accompaniment (‘with’) and سواری here means ‘riders/horsemen’ (a mounted force); the verb from the previous clause (بیامد) is understood to extend to this coordinated phrase, so the phrase reads ‘and Rozbeh with a thousand horsemen (came).’
segments.s611-g3: همه دشت is a noun phrase ‘the whole plain’ (همه used as ‘whole/entire’). یکسر is an adverb ‘completely/entirely’ modifying پر, and پر از گور is the construction ‘full of graves’ (پر + از + noun). دید is the past tense ‘he saw’ and is the main verb of the clause.
segments.s611-g4: ز here is the poetic variant of از meaning ‘from’ or ‘out of’. قربان is used as ‘sacrifice’/‘the slain’ (object or setting from which something is taken). کمان کیان means ‘the bow of the Kians’ (کیان = the royal/Kayan house) with the possessive relation expressed without the ezāfe in elevated/poetic style. برکشید is the past of برکشیدن, here ‘drew/raised’ (i.e., drew the bow), a transitive past action.
segments.s611-g5: دو زاغ is the direct object phrase ‘two crows’ (numeral + noun). کمان را here is a definite noun phrase marked with the object marker را but functioning with the following locative phrase; poetically it denotes ‘the bow’ as the scene. به زه = ‘on the string’ (به marking motion/placement onto), and بر نهاد is the past form of نهادن with the prefix بر‑ indicating placing upon; together they mean ‘he placed/laid (them) on the bowstring.’
segments.s611-g6: ز یزدان is an ablative/agent phrase ‘by/from God’ (ز = poetic از). پیروزگر follows یزدان as an epithet meaning ‘the victorious’ (the victorious God). کرد is the past of کردن ‘to make/do’, and یاد is the object ‘remembrance/memory’ — so the clause says that the victorious God ‘made it a remembrance’ or caused it to be remembered.
segments.s611-g7: بهاران و گوران is a coordinated noun phrase (‘the springs and the graves’ or ‘spring and graves’) used as the subject. شده is the past participle/auxiliary ‘have become.’ جفت جوی is a compound nominal predicate ‘paired/twin streams’ (جفت modifying جوی). The clause thus says that life (spring) and death (graves) have alike turned into paired streams — a poetic image.
segments.s611-g8: ز کشتن is a circumstantial noun phrase ‘from/because of the killing’ with کشتن as a verbal noun (the slaughter). به روی اندر آورده روی is a compressed poetic expression: به روی ‘to/onto faces’, اندر آورده (آورده with the enclitic اندر) ‘have brought/turned’, and the repeated روی intensifies ‘faces (upon faces)’ — overall ‘by the slaughter they have been brought face to face’ (i.e., corpses or peoples brought face‑to‑face as a result of killing).

sentences.s611-s13: There was a powerful man in the new age who was at once king, sovereign, and ruler of the orb.
sentences.s611-s9: He shot an arrow into the back of that male onager; the arrow's shaft and feather passed over it.
sentences.s611-s10: He pierced both the male and the female together; the army's hearts were set aflame by that wound of his.
sentences.s611-s11: From the host, whoever saw that wound showered praise upon that sovereign.
sentences.s611-s12: May the evil eye be far from your farr (royal glory); may all your days be a feast.
sentences.s611-s5: They kept flaying one another — this one from that, that one from this; from their blood the surface of the earth became ruby-red.
sentences.s611-s6: Bahram kept on until the male onager, in frenzy, was separated from the other.
sentences.s611-s7: When the brave male onager was victorious, he brought one female beneath (him).
sentences.s611-s8: Bahram held a war‑bow with a string and laughed when the onager became joyful.
sentences.s611-s1: On the eighth day he came to his hunting plain, and Rozbeh came with a thousand horsemen.
sentences.s611-s2: He saw the whole plain entirely full of graves; then he drew the Kayan bow from the sacrifice.
sentences.s611-s3: He set two crows upon the bow’s string; the victorious God made it a remembrance.
sentences.s611-s4: Spring and graves alike have become twin streams; by slaughter they have brought face to face.
